summary: Cathy O'Neil examines research by Seena Fazel revealing that predictive risk models used to assess prisoners with mental disorders are deeply flawed, performing barely better than a coin flip for the most violent populations. These models are widely used across the US, UK, and other countries to make consequential decisions about indefinite detention and treatment. The high false-positive rates mean some offenders spend longer incarcerated than their actual risk warrants. O'Neil argues these tools provide a false veneer of scientific credibility that leads to over-reliance by courts and clinicians. She calls for transparency, open models, and a return to behavior-based methodology rather than trusting flawed algorithmic predictions.
key_points: Predictive risk models for violent and sexual offenders are already in widespread use across multiple countries, influencing decisions about indefinite detention
Meta-analysis shows these models perform poorly, especially for the highest-risk populations they're supposedly designed to assess
High false-positive rates result in people being incarcerated longer than their true risk level justifies
The 'veneer of scientific respectability' leads courts and clinicians to over-rely on flawed tools
O'Neil advocates for open models with protected data sets to enable proper scrutiny
money_quotes: In practice, the high false-positive rate probably means that some offenders spend longer in prison and secure hospital than their true risk would suggest.
This seems to be yet another example of a mathematical obfuscation and intimidation that gives people a false sense of having a good tool at hand.
It doesn't make sense to put people in jail an extra 10 years because a crappy model said so.
The veneer of scientific respectability surrounding such methods may lead to over-reliance on their findings.
The data itself is extremely sensitive and protected, but the model itself should be scrutinized.
key_insight: Mathematical models used in criminal justice create a dangerous illusion of scientific precision that masks their fundamental unreliability, leading to unjust extended incarceration.
